title_en: Zakat, Fasting, and Hajj in Fath al-Mu'in
title_ar: فصل: Zakat, Fasting, and Hajj in Fath al-Mu'in
title_id: Zakat, Puasa, dan Haji dalam Fath al-Mu'in
content_en:
The chapters on the financial and ritual pillars in Fath al-Mu'in demonstrate al-Malibari's skill at presenting Shafi'i law at a level that is both practically complete and intellectually honest about the complexity of the issues.

The zakah chapter covers all the standard categories with enough detail for practical application. On agricultural produce, al-Malibari addresses the question of rice — the staple crop of the Indian Ocean world — applying the Shafi'i principle that crops subject to zakah are those measured by volume and capable of being stored as a staple food. Rice clearly qualifies, and al-Malibari gives the applicable rate: one-tenth for rain-watered crops and one-twentieth for crops dependent on irrigation.

For zakah on trade goods, the work addresses the questions that arose in the active trading communities of the Malabar coast: goods held in different currencies, goods in transit, goods partially owned by a Muslim partner, and goods whose value fluctuates significantly through the year. Al-Malibari applies the Shafi'i principles — assessment at the end of the hawl at current market value — to these specific commercial scenarios.

The fasting chapter in Fath al-Mu'in covers Ramadan fasting and the voluntary fasts recommended by the Sunnah. On the intention for Ramadan fasting, al-Malibari confirms the Shafi'i requirement of a nightly intention, explaining why the Shafi'i school insists on this against the Hanafi allowance of a general monthly intention: each day of Ramadan is a separate act of worship in the Shafi'i view, requiring its own intention.

The chapter on the nullifiers of fasting is carefully organized. Al-Malibari distinguishes between nullifiers that require qada' only and those that additionally require kaffarah. For the Shafi'i school, kaffarah (freeing a slave, then fasting two months, then feeding sixty poor persons) applies specifically to deliberate sexual intercourse during a day of Ramadan that one was obligated to fast. Other deliberate nullifiers require qada' but not kaffarah.

On hajj, Fath al-Mu'in addresses the conditions of the obligation with attention to the specific situation of people in the Indian Ocean world, for whom the journey to Mecca was a major undertaking. The Shafi'i principle that the obligation requires 'ability' (istita'ah) — including financial capacity, physical fitness, and secure passage — is applied with awareness that the conditions for travelers from India or Southeast Asia differed significantly from those for residents of the Arabian Peninsula.
content_id:
Bab-bab tentang rukun-rukun finansial dan ritual dalam Fath al-Mu'in menunjukkan keahlian al-Malibari dalam menyajikan hukum Syafi'i pada tingkat yang sekaligus lengkap secara praktis dan jujur secara intelektual tentang kerumitan persoalan-persoalan tersebut.

Bab zakat mencakup semua kategori standar dengan detail yang cukup untuk penerapan praktis. Mengenai hasil pertanian, al-Malibari membahas masalah beras sebagai tanaman pokok dunia Samudra Hindia dengan menerapkan prinsip Syafi'i bahwa tanaman yang wajib dizakati adalah yang diukur dengan volume dan dapat disimpan sebagai makanan pokok. Beras jelas memenuhi syarat, dan al-Malibari memberikan kadar yang berlaku: sepersepuluh untuk tanaman yang diairi hujan dan seperduapuluh untuk tanaman yang bergantung pada irigasi.

Untuk zakat perdagangan, kitab ini membahas pertanyaan-pertanyaan yang muncul di komunitas dagang yang aktif di pesisir Malabar: barang yang dipegang dalam berbagai mata uang, barang dalam perjalanan, barang yang sebagian dimiliki oleh mitra Muslim, dan barang yang nilainya berfluktuasi secara signifikan sepanjang tahun. Al-Malibari menerapkan prinsip-prinsip Syafi'i yaitu penilaian di akhir haul berdasarkan harga pasar saat ini pada skenario perdagangan khusus ini.

Bab puasa dalam Fath al-Mu'in mencakup puasa Ramadan dan puasa sunnah yang dianjurkan oleh Sunnah. Mengenai niat puasa Ramadan, al-Malibari menegaskan persyaratan mazhab Syafi'i tentang niat malam hari, menjelaskan mengapa mazhab Syafi'i bersikeras tentang hal ini berbeda dengan kebolehan Hanafi tentang niat bulanan yang bersifat umum: setiap hari Ramadan adalah ibadah yang terpisah menurut pandangan Syafi'i, yang memerlukan niatnya sendiri.

Bab tentang pembatal puasa disusun dengan cermat. Al-Malibari membedakan antara pembatal yang hanya mengharuskan qada dan pembatal yang juga mengharuskan kafarat. Bagi mazhab Syafi'i, kafarat yaitu memerdekakan budak, kemudian berpuasa dua bulan, kemudian memberi makan enam puluh orang miskin berlaku khusus untuk hubungan seksual yang disengaja pada hari Ramadan yang wajib dipuasai. Pembatal disengaja lainnya mengharuskan qada tetapi tidak kafarat.

Mengenai haji, Fath al-Mu'in membahas syarat-syarat kewajiban dengan memperhatikan situasi khusus orang-orang di dunia Samudra Hindia yang perjalanan ke Mekah merupakan upaya besar bagi mereka. Prinsip Syafi'i bahwa kewajiban memerlukan kemampuan (istitha'ah) termasuk kemampuan finansial, kebugaran fisik, dan jalur yang aman diterapkan dengan kesadaran bahwa kondisi bagi para musafir dari India atau Asia Tenggara berbeda secara signifikan dari kondisi penduduk Jazirah Arab.
content_ar: The الأبواب on the financial and ritual pillars in Fath al-Mu'in demonstrate al-Malibari's skill at presenting الشافعي law at a level that is both practically complete and intellectually honest about the complexity of the issues. The الزكاة الباب covers all the standard categories with enough detail for practical application. On agricultural produce, al-Malibari addresses the question of rice — the staple crop of the Indian Ocean الدنيا — applying the الشافعي principle that crops subject to الزكاة are those measured by volume and capable of being stored as a staple food. Rice clearly qualifies, and al-Malibari gives the applicable rate: one-عاشراً for rain-watered crops and one-twentieth for crops dependent on irrigation. For الزكاة on trade goods, the work addresses the questions that arose in the active trading communities of the Malabar coast: goods held in different currencies, goods in transit, goods partially owned by a مسلم partner, and goods whose value fluctuates significantly through the year. Al-Malibari applies the الشافعي principles — assessment at the end of the hawl at current market value — to these specific commercial scenarios. The الصيام الباب in Fath al-Mu'in covers Ramadan الصيام and the voluntary fasts recommended by the السنة. On the intention for Ramadan الصيام, al-Malibari confirms the الشافعي requirement of a nightly intention, explaining why the الشافعي school insists on this against the الحنفي allowance of a general monthly intention: each day of Ramadan is a separate act of worship in the الشافعي view, requiring its own intention. The الباب on the nullifiers of الصيام is carefully organized. Al-Malibari distinguishes between nullifiers that require القضاء' only and those that additionally require kaffarah. For the الشافعي school, kaffarah (freeing a slave, then الصيام two months, then feeding sixty poor persons) applies specifically to deliberate sexual intercourse خلال a day of Ramadan that one was obligated to fast. Other deliberate nullifiers require القضاء' but not kaffarah. On الحج, Fath al-Mu'in addresses the conditions of the الالتزام with attention to the specific situation of people in the Indian Ocean الدنيا, for whom the journey to مكة المكرمة was a major undertaking. The الشافعي principle that the الالتزام requires 'ability' (istita'ah) — بما في ذلك financial capacity, physical fitness, and secure passage — is applied with awareness that the conditions for travelers from India or Southeast Asia differed significantly from those for residents of the Arabian Peninsula.
